coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue using carbon dots-modified copper chloride hydroxide nanocomposite
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Meng-Jie
Jiang, Jian-Zhe
Song, Zong-He
Ren, Tie-Zhen
coredata.dc:description: The dye organic pollutants with utilization in chemical industries have been discharged into the environment, causing the water pollution and human disease. Therefore, developing a cheap, reliable and sustainable wastewater cleaning technology is of great importance. Transitional metal is often one of the active photocatalysts for degradation of dye pollution. Among them, Cu has attracted attention due to low price and catalytic activity. In this article, we took Cu sheets as raw materials and used a simple salting-out method to prepare Cu2Cl(OH)3 nanocomposites, which exhibited a photocurrent response under the UV light. At the same time, the structural transformation process from Cu sheet to nanoparticle Cu2Cl(OH)3 was observed over time. The results showed that Cu2Cl(OH)3 displayed significant photocurrent response. In addition, in order to further improve its catalytic performance, Cu2Cl(OH)3 was modified by different concentrations of carbon dots (CDs) and a wide UV light absorption received. Corresponding to it, 1CDs@OHCu was efficient to the photodegradation of methylene blue (MB) within 3 h compared to the mother sample. The physical properties of prepared samples were studied through XRD, UV–vis spectra, SEM, etc. The related kinetic rate of the process of degrading MB was recorded to determine the role of CDs. The intermediates produced during the photo-reaction was monitored via the chemical trapping agent. By analyzing the characteristics and electrochemical features of prepared samples, the catalytic active center was explored. Finally, stability analysis showed that 1CDs@OHCu could be recycled at least 8 times.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper compound
Salting method
Photocatalysis
Carbon dots modification

Natural Science Foundation of Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region ( 2021D01A03 ) and Key Research and Development Projects of Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region (2022B02038 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis route for xCDs@OHCu material.
SEM images of Cu (a) and OHCu (b) at low resolution.
(a) UV–visible absorption spectra of OHCu before and after modification by CDs; (b) photocurrent test curves of OHCu before and after CDs modification under UV light.
(a) MB degradation with OHCu and 1CDs@OHCu under neutral conditions; (b) Cyclic stability test of MB degradation solution by 1CDs@OHCu.
The Mott-Schottky curves of OHCu (a) and 1CDs@OHCu (b) at different frequencies; (c) Nyquist plot of OHCu and 1CDs@OHCu.
(a)MB degradation by 1CDs@OHCu under UV light after adding scavenger; (b) Photocatalytic degradation mechanism of 1CDs@OHCu.
Comparison of different reported photocatalysts for photodegradation of MB.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The efficiency was calculated at 1 h.

head.ce:title: Enhanced photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue using carbon dots-modified copper chloride hydroxide nanocomposite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources and Key Laboratory of Coal Clean Conversion & Chemical Engineering Process (Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region), College of Chemical Engineering, Xinjiang University, Urumqi, Xinjiang 830046, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources and Key Laboratory of Coal Clean Conversion & Chemical Engineering Process (Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region), College of Chemical Engineering, Xinjiang University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources and Key Laboratory of Coal Clean Conversion & Chemical Engineering Process (Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region), College of Chemical Engineering, Xinjiang University, Urumqi, Xinjiang 830046, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin 300130, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin 300130, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemical Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin 300130, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The salting-out extraction method was used for Cu2Cl(OH)3 preparation through the structure transformation of copper. The carbon dots modification could enhance the photoresponse of prepared nonocomposites, contributing to the high separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes. The photodegradation rate of methylene blue was improved from 37.4% to 88.2% after carbon modification. By adding the scavenger agents, the ·O2 – radicals was proved as major intermediates during photocatalysis.

The dye organic pollutants with utilization in chemical industries have been discharged into the environment, causing the water pollution and human disease. Therefore, developing a cheap, reliable and sustainable wastewater cleaning technology is of great importance. Transitional metal is often one of the active photocatalysts for degradation of dye pollution. Among them, Cu has attracted attention due to low price and catalytic activity. In this article, we took Cu sheets as raw materials and used a simple salting-out method to prepare Cu2Cl(OH)3 nanocomposites, which exhibited a photocurrent response under the UV light. At the same time, the structural transformation process from Cu sheet to nanoparticle Cu2Cl(OH)3 was observed over time. The results showed that Cu2Cl(OH)3 displayed significant photocurrent response. In addition, in order to further improve its catalytic performance, Cu2Cl(OH)3 was modified by different concentrations of carbon dots (CDs) and a wide UV light absorption received. Corresponding to it, 1CDs@OHCu was efficient to the photodegradation of methylene blue (MB) within 3 h compared to the mother sample. The physical properties of prepared samples were studied through XRD, UV–vis spectra, SEM, etc. The related kinetic rate of the process of degrading MB was recorded to determine the role of CDs. The intermediates produced during the photo-reaction was monitored via the chemical trapping agent. By analyzing the characteristics and electrochemical features of prepared samples, the catalytic active center was explored. Finally, stability analysis showed that 1CDs@OHCu could be recycled at least 8 times.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past decades, wastewater induced by organic dye pollutants has become a fatal issue accompanying the rapid industry growth, which restricted the sustainable development of human beings [1–3]. Among them, the discharge of toxic substances from textile and dyeing industries is one of the important sources of water pollution. In particular, dyes are mixed with water resources without proper purification, and dye-containing wastewater is toxic and carcinogenic. These are considered to be an important source of environmental pollution [4]. Therefore, we need to develop an effective method for the removal of dye from wastewater. Currently, the green photocatalytic process has attracted more attention as the most promising method for the removal of dye pollutants [5,6]. A great effort has been made for seeking highly active photocatalysts in environmental remediation [7,8].

Copper-based photocatalyst is widely used in wastewater treatment thanks to its abundant reserves, low price and high photocatalytic efficiency [9]. Manisha et al. [10] reported a facile, simple microwave assisted synthesis of Cu2O microcubes, with which the Congo red could be degraded in visible-light. Huang et al. [11] prepared the Fe3O4/Cu2O/Au nanocomposites through wet-chemical and in-situ reduction reactions. An obvious degradation of MB was observed in Cu2O contained system. While Cu+ is metastable and easy to be oxidized to Cu2+. CuO/TiO2 composites displayed an enhanced photoresponse and the removal efficiency for toluene increased from 60 to 80 % under visible light, attributing to a narrow bandgap of CuO [12]. CuS/ZnO nanocomposite obtained by a simple two-step solution approach was efficient to the photodegradation of Congo red and phenol [13]. Compared with pure ZnO, the photocatalytic efficiency of ZnO/CuS composite improved to 82 %, with an augmentation of 28 %. However, most of the current research focuses on Cu metal, oxides or sulfides, few reports on other Cu compounds.

The copper oxychloride Cu2Cl(OH)3 is sensitive to the light for photoelectron generation and with a narrow bandgap. Yang et al. [3] prepared uniform nanoparticles Cu2Cl(OH)3 by H2O2-assisted hydrothermal synthesis and 98.5 % of Rhodamine B (RhB) was degraded after 30 min under mild acidic condition (pH = 5.0). The CuO/Cu2Cl(OH)3 powder was prepared through CuCl2 and NaOH for the photodegradation of Amidoblack10B and Indigo carmine [14]. Above 90 % degradation was achieved after 4 h light irradiation. Ye et al. [15] successfully prepared Al2O3 fibre evenly loaded with CeO2/CuO by salting-out extraction method. With the increase of Cu content in the loading substance, the degradation of what by it reached to 89.71 % after 5 h of illumination. The result further proved that Cu2+ obtained by impregnation method had certain photocatalytic degradation performance. Which is also facile and east operating technique. While, the advanced photocatalysts are in a high demand for complete and fast pollutants degradation. Heteroatoms doping is a good strategy to improve the photocatalysis of metal oxides. For example, carbon dots (CDs) modified materials could effectively separate photo-generated electrons/holes to promote the reaction [16–20]. The up-conversion of light absorption could be intensified as its inherent surface defects is favorable to stabilize the photocatalyst [21–24].

In this work, Cu-based photocatalyst was produced through precipitation in a neutral salt solution. The structural transformation is time dependent till a stable copper compound Cu2Cl(OH)3 (OHCu) formed. In order to further improve its photocatalytic performance, CDs was introduced through a simple immersing process at the ambient circumstance to obtain 1CDs@OHCu. Through various analytical studies, the relationship between reaction time and structural transformation, morphology, optical properties, and physicochemical properties were studied in detail. It was found that 1CDs@OHCu catalyst exhibited effective degradation performance for MB dye under UV irradiation. With the help of chemical trapping agents, the intermediate active center during photodegradation was monitored, with which a possible photocatalytic pass way was revealed and the possible mechanism was clarified.

Sodium chloride (NaCl) and methylene blue dye (C16H18CIN3S) were bought from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai) Co., Ltd with analytical pure grade and used exactly as received with no further purification. The Cu sheet and CDs were provided by Xinjiang Hongruida Co., Ltd. CDs solution (1 %) was obtained from the pyrolyzed effluent lignin solution.

Cu2Cl(OH)3 photocatalyst was prepared by the salting-out extraction method. First, 0.89 g of Cu sheet (1 cm × 1 cm × 0.1 mm) was immediately placed into distilled water and anhydrous ethanol to ultrasonically clean for 30 min and finally dry for using. Then the Cu sheets were immersed in 80 ml of NaCl solution (1 mol/L) at room temperature for days, accompanying with the change of transparent solution to light green. After 7 days of reaction, the Cu sheet was removed and centrifuged at 2000 r min−1 for 30 min. The collected light green powder was washed with distilled water for 3 times and dried at 60 °C to obtain the final product, which was named as OHCu. During the salting-out extraction, no particle could be collected from the Cu sheet, but the colour of Cu sheet was dark gradually in 7 days. For understanding the transformation of copper compound, Cu sheet was collected and named as Cu-xd (x is the number of immersion days).

10 mg of OHCu was added into the 10 ml of CDs solution (0.5, 1, 5, 10 mg/L) with an ultrasonic treatment for 10 min to form a suspension. Then, the suspension was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, followed by heating at 100 °C for 2 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solid samples were separated by centrifugation. Further washing was performed in the distilled water for several times and placed samples in an oven at 60 °C. The collected samples were referred to xCDs@OHCu (x is the concentration of CDs). The synthesis route was described in Fig. 1 .

The structural information of prepared sample was revealed from the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern using a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.154 nm) at 40 kV and 40 mA. The morphology of the samples was observed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-6490LV). Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy (Hitachi SU8010) was used in analyzing the elemental composition and the sample was dispersed on the Si substrate. Using UV–Vis spectral analysis (TU-1901) with deuterium lamp and tungsten lamp as light source and a scanning range of 230–800 nm, the absorption curves of samples for photons at different wavelengths were obtained. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Nicolet iS20) was used to probe the vibrational groups of samples.

Working electrode was prepared with 0.05 g of xCDs@OHCu, mixed with 12.5 wt% of acetylene black, 12.5 wt% of polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) in the solvent of N-methyl pyrrolidone. A homogenous mixture was coated directly on commercial Ti foil (1 cm × 1 cm), which was ultrasonic cleaned in ethanol and deionized water before use.

The electrochemical measurements, including Mott-Schottky (M−S) test and electrochemical impedance test (EIS), were recorded with the electrochemical workstation (Zahner ZENNIUM IM6) at room temperature. The three-electrode system was used with Ag/AgCl electrode as reference electrode, Pt foil (2 cm × 2 cm) as the counter electrode. The electrolyte was set in 1 M of KOH solution.

The photocurrent test electrode was prepared as follows. 1 mg of the sample powder and 3 ml of ethanol were mixed with ultrasonic dispersion for 30 min. Then 50 μL of the suspension was uniformly dropped on the surface of prepared indium tin oxide (ITO) (2 cm × 2 cm) conductive glass. After the membrane was formed on ITO substrate, cleaning with distilled water and ethanol in sequence for 1 h. Finally, the electrode was dried in an oven at 60 °C and an effective area of 2 cm2 covered with sample was obtained.

The photocurrent response was measured by electrochemical workstation (mode PP211) in a standard three-electrode system at room temperature. Ag/AgCl was the reference electrode and Pt foil (2 cm × 2 cm) was the counter electrode. The sodium sulfate solution (0.2 mol/L) was the electrolyte. The white light emitting diode with the intensity of 10 mW·cm2 was used as the light source, and the switching state of the light source is switched every 30 s.

The photocatalytic activity of the synthesized catalyst was evaluated by measuring the catalytic degradation efficiency of MB under ultraviolet light, and the degradation of MB was measured by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The concentration of MB with time was calculated by Beer-Lambert law [25].
(1)
C t
/
C 0
=
A t
/
A 0
× 100 %
Where, Ct/C0 reflects the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of MB, according to the varying of absorbance value At/A0 .
In addition, the cyclic stability test of the photocatalyst was carried out for 8 times within 24 h.

The surface change of the copper sheet was observed with the immersion time. The SEM image of the original Cu sheet presented in Fig. 2 a shows a rough surface and tiny crevice. The collected powder exhibits well-shaped particles in microscale (Fig. 2b). In between the copper sheet and powder sample, the Cu-3d shows the obviously distorted surface (Fig. S1a). In Cu-4d, the nanoparticles in cube/brick shape appear on the Cu sheet surface with a size less than 100 nm (Fig. S1b). Cu-5d represented in Fig. S1c shows the large shaped particles among the infinite nanoparticles. This salt immersion procedure took 7 days for getting the powder sample. In Fig. S2 OHCu shows several sharp peaks, indicating a high crystallinity [26]. Those diffraction peaks could be indexed as JCPDS No. 50–1559, which is coincident with Cu2Cl(OH)3 (a = 0.6157 nm, b = 0.6814 nm, c = 0.9104 nm, α = γ = 90°, β = 99.65°) [3,27–28]. Cu-3d is still indexed as Cu metal (JCPDS No. 04–0836), but the crystal plane of (200) at 50.43° becomes weak and plane (111) at 43.27° is obvious stronger than before, suggesting that the (200) plane possesses a weak energy and the inner plane exposed [29]. In Cu-4d, the peaks at 28.52°, 47.44° and 56.29° are indexed to (111), (220) and (311) plane of CuCl (JCPDS No. 06–0344) [30]. Further on Cu-5d, the peaks at 16.19°, 32.63° and 39.74° can be ascribed to Cu2Cl(OH)3 (JCPDS No. 50–1599), fitting to the crystal plane of (011), (013) and (220), respectively, confirming that copper sheet could be transformed to Cu2Cl(OH)3 with salting-immersion.

FTIR was performed to clarify the effect of the CDs to Cu2Cl(OH)3 (Fig. S3a). CDs curve shows a broad peak at 3432 cm−1, fixing to the stretching vibration of OH and intramolecular hydrogen bonds [31]. The characteristic peaks at 2942 cm−1 and 1586 cm−1 belong to the vibration absorption of the CH bonds and the CO bonds [32–33]. The peak at 1424 cm−1 can be attributed to COO– absorption bands [34]. The peaks at 1116 cm−1 and 1046 cm−1 indicate that CO exists on the surface of CDs [35]. The vibration absorption of CC bonds and bendingmodes of sp2 CH are represented at 765 cm−1 [36]. The peak at 658 cm−1 is a resonance peak that can be assigned to the out-of-plane bendingmodes of CH and CN [37]. For Cu2Cl(OH)3, the peaks nearat 3297 and 3446 cm−1 correspond to the OH lattice stretching vibration mode. The peak at 800–1000 cm−1 can be attributed to the δı and ʋ1 (CuOH) vibrations of Cu2Cl(OH)3 [38–39]. After doping CDs, CDs@OHCu also displayed the feature peaks of Cu2Cl(OH)3, suggesting that no chemical bonding between the molecules of CDs and Cu2Cl(OH)3 was formed. The small CDs particles might hide among Cu2Cl(OH)3, this physical mixture would not affect the microstructure of the samples. Then we observed the morphology of CDs to confirm the particle size of CDs. As represented in Fig. S3b, the nanoparticles are around 1.5 to 3.5 nm. It is much smaller than the Cu2Cl(OH)3 particle and helpful for increasing the contact area, which further increases the formation rate of catalytic intermediates [40–41].

To further confirm the surface chemical environment of the CDs, the XPS spectrum was carried out. As shown in Fig. S4a, CDs consists of C (85.37 at-%), N (0.28 at-%) and O (14.35 at-%) elements. Based on the deconvolution of C 1s (Fig. S4b), CC (284.6 eV) content is the highest, up to 78.51 %. The other peaks at 285.7 eV, 286.6 eV and 288.6 eV, respectively are corresponding to CN (11.34 %), CO (7.54 %) and CC (2.61 %). The peak at 399.95 eV in N1s (Fig. S4c) represents pyrrole N. The deconvolution of O 1s (22.79 at-%) in Fig. S4d displays three contributions at 531.9, 532.7 and 533.6 eV, corresponding to CO, CO and OH in the sample. The content of CO is the highest (46.96 %), followed by CO (39.89 %) and OH (13.15 %). The results show that the types of chemical bonds are consistent with the results of FTIR.

To estimate the atomic percentage of the sample. The elemental composition of 1CDs@OHCu was analyzed using EDX spectroscopy, as listed in Table S1 and Fig. S5. The carbon (∼2.98 %), oxygen (55.98 %), chlorine (4.79 %) and copper (36.25 %) atoms were observed. Based on the EDX result, the higher oxygen percentage might fit to COO–, CO and CO groups on the surface of CD. These functional groups might contribute to the activation of electron, enhancing the degradation activity of photocatalysts [42].

Thus the UV–vis spectra was recorded to reveal the photoresponse of Cu2Cl(OH)3. The OHCu represents a broad, but weak absorbance at 200–500 nm. After CDs doping, the absorbance increases rapidly (Fig. 3 a). However, the more CDs content, the lower absorption intensity, indicating that the overloaded CDs on the OHCu surface prevents from its exposing to light, diminishing its light absorption [43]. In addition, a blue-shift after CDs modification at 300–400 nm appears and 1CDs@OHCu displays the strongest absorption capacity.

Then the photocurrent response under UV light were tested to reveal the contribution of CDs. As represented in Fig. 3b, the photocurrent density of 1CDs@OHCu shows a leapfrog growth, which is much higher than that of others and almost triple times to OHCu. Meanwhile, the variation of photocurrent intensity is consistent to the UV–vis absorbance value, giving an advanced confirmation that the optimized CDs doping could promote the photoresponse of Cu2Cl(OH)3. This photocatalytic performance might be applied in degradation of MB.

The degradation process might be ascribed to the first-order kinetic equation.Fig. S7 shows the kinetic curves of the photocatalytic degradation of MB by OHCu and 1CDs@OHCu. The rate constant ‘k’ value is determined for the photocatalysts [52]. With OHCu the value of ‘k’ is 0.00263 min−1, whereas the value of ‘k’ with 1CDs@OHCu increases to 0.01173 min−1. This four times enlarged value suggests a significant sacrifice of CDs for the photodegradation of MB.

The cyclingstability results of the CDs-modified catalyst 1CDs@OHCu sample were represented in Fig. 4b. Under the same conditions, the MB degradation rate did not decrease significantly after 8 cycles. At the end of each catalytic process, the maximum fluctuation value of the MB removal rate is around 0.7 %, implying its good cyclingstability.

We collected the sample after each degradation experiment and the degradation percentage was calculated in each run. The stability and recyclability of 1CDs@OHCu towards MB dye photodegradation were evaluated after eight consecutive runs and the obtained data are presented in Fig. 4b. Obviously, the maintained structure after photocatalysis was evidenced by the XRD pattern (Fig. S8). There is no change in the structure of the sample before degradation, but with a decreased intensity. It represents the high stability of 1CDs@OHCu. The M−S curve generally describes the semiconductor characteristics of catalysts, of which the slope is positive for n-type semiconductors with free electrons as charge carriers and negative is for p-type semiconductors with holes as charge carriers [53,54]. The tested frequency is set at 100, 200, 500, 1000 and 2000 Hz. We can see that both of 1CDs@OHCu and OHCu are n-type semiconductors as shown in Fig. 5 a and 5b. While, the globalnumerical value of 1CDs@OHCu increases significantly at the same frequency, indicating that the number of free electrons increases with CDs modification [55]. This mightprofit the reaction of electron and dissolved oxygen in water, producing efficient intermediates with dye mineralization.

Meanwhile, the influence of CDs modification on the electron transfer rate could be reflected by the test of EIS (Fig. 5c). The intercepts at high frequency, with the real axis belongs to an equivalent series resistance (Rs), that is the total resistances of the electrode (Relectrode), the electrolyte resistance (Relectrolyte) and the resistance at the electrolyte/electrode interface (Rinterface). The width of the semi-circle reflects the charge-transfer resistance (Rct) [56]. The semi-circle in a high-frequency region of the 1CDs@OHCu catalyst is significantly smaller than that of OHCu, indicating that CDs modification can effectively improve the electron transfer rate of the catalyst.

To gain the mechanistic pathway of photocatalytic degradation of MB by 1CDs@OHCu, different radical scavenger was chosen. The tert-butanol (TBA) is for hydroxyl radical (·OH), ammonium oxalate (AO) is used as hole (hVB +) scavenger, and cerium nitrate (Ce(NO3)3) is used as superoxide radical ion (·O2 –) scavenger[57]. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the photocatalytic degradation rate of the catalystisalmostunchanged when introducing TBA and AO. Thus ·OH and hVB + have little effect on degradation of MB. However, the degradation rate is obviously limited by the addition of Ce(NO3)3. The remained concentration of MB increased from 11.8 % to 81.9 % after 3 h, suggesting that the ·O2 – plays a major role in the photocatalytic degradation of dye. The electrons could reduce the dissolved O2 in water, generating the intermediate ·O2 – of the photodegradation process [58].

Hence under UV illumination, electrons in the valence band of OHCu absorb the energy of the photon transferring to the conduction band and producing excited electrons, which then inducing strong oxidizing intermediate ·O2 – for MB reduction. After releasing energy, the electrons return to the valence band to prepare for the next reaction process (Fig. 6b). Meanwhile, the photogenerated h+ on the VB of CDs can directly oxidize the H2O/OH– absorbed on its surface to generate ·OH radicals [59–61] and give a tinyeffect on the reduction of MB.

The salt immersing technique is a simple and ambient route. The reaction process in salt solution involved the participation of O2 and H2O that could be concluded in reaction equations (1) and (2). When the solution colour changed from colourless to light green, no new substance was generated in the beginning. With the time passing, Cu is slowly partially oxidized to CuCl by O2 in the presence of Cl- (reaction (1). However, CuCl is unstable and further oxidized with the formation of Cu2Cl(OH)3 (reaction (2). Finally, the pH value of the solution increased and the Cu sheet gradually thinned until it disappeared, and a large amount of light green powder appeared in the solution, which corresponds to the total equation (3).
(1)
4 C u +
O 2
+ 4
Cl
-
+ 2
H 2
O → 4 C u C l + 4
OH
-
(2)
4 C u C l +
O 2

+ 2

(3)

H 2

O 2
+ 2

C l
( O H )
3
+ 2
OH
-

CDs modification can increase the lifetime of photogenerated electrons in the catalyst and reduce their recombination with holes. The rich superficial functional group of CDs can also make it form a conjugated structure with organic matter and enhance the organic waste that is adsorbed on the catalyst surface, further accelerating the rate of O2 reduction by photogenerated electrons [62–64]. The modification of CDs isbeneficialto the transfer of photogenerated electrons in the catalyst, which leads to accelerate the separation of photogenerated electrons from holes and improves the quantum efficiency of the catalyst.

In this paper, 1CDs@OHCu photocatalyst was prepared first by the salting-out extraction and then the hydrothermal synthesize method. The characterizations using various techniques were discussed. The optical band gap of 1CDs@OHCu nanocomposites has been significantly tuned and the electrochemical tests were investigated to reveal the effects of CDs on the photocatalytic of OHCu. As a result, the maximum degradation efficiency of MB was achieved to 88.2 % within 180 min under UV irradiation. The high stability was approved with 8 cycling tests. The trapping experiment to the active center confirmed that ·O2 – radical was the major species for the degradation of MB in the photocatalytic process. We confirmed that 1CDs@OHCu nanocomposites system had a stronger ability to transmit electrons which enabled to promote more effective separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, as compared to other samples. Therefore, 1CDs@OHCu displayed the better photodegradation performance, which could be applied in the field of other dye removal treatment and environmental remediation activities.

Meng-Jie Cui: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Jian-Zhe Jiang: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Zong-He Song: Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Tie-Zhen Ren: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region (2021D01A03) and Key Research and Development Projects of Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region (2022B02038).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2023.111559.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced dye degradation performance enabled by swift electron mediator decorated WO3/g-C3N4/V2O5 hybrid nanomaterials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Ouyang et al., “Recent advances in photocatalytic degradation of plastics and plastic-derived chemicals,” Journal of Materials Chemistry A, vol. 9, no. 23, pp. 13402–13441, 2021, doi: 10.1039/D0TA12465F.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in crystalline carbon dots for superior application potential
Mater. Adv.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Pal, M. P. Sk, and A. Chattopadhyay, “Recent advances in crystalline carbon dots for superior application potential,” Materials Advances, vol. 1, no. 4, pp. 525–553, 2020, doi: 10.1039/D0MA00108B.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Oxygen-defective ZnO porous nanosheets modified by carbon dots to improve their visible-light photocatalytic activity and gain mechanistic insight
New J. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Guo, H. Sun, L. Cheng, and W. Shi, “Oxygen-defective ZnO porous nanosheets modified by carbon dots to improve their visible-light photocatalytic activity and gain mechanistic insight,” New Journal of Chemistry, vol. 44, no. 26, pp. 11215–11223, 2020, doi: 10.1039/D0NJ02268C.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Boosted photocatalytic activity of LaFeO3/Ag3PO4 heterojunction via carbon quantum dots: Higher conductivity, stability, and dispersivity
Colloids Surf. A Physicochem. Eng. Asp.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Li, L. Fan, X. Shui, J. Fan, X. Feng, and T. Tao, “Boosted photocatalytic activity of LaFeO3/Ag3PO4 heterojunction via carbon quantum dots: Higher conductivity, stability, and dispersivity,” Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects, vol. 652, p. 129895, Nov. 2022, doi: 10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.129895.
